transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hi Naomi, I’m glad you’re here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Um, I guess... I don't know. There are a lot of things, but I’m not sure if any of them are really worth discussing. Sometimes it just feels like it’s all the same stuff, you know? I guess I could talk about work, but it’s just... complicated. I don’t want to get into trouble or anything.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you're feeling a bit overwhelmed with everything that's going on, and there's a sense of hesitation about discussing work because of potential consequences. It’s completely understandable to feel that way. Can you share a little more about what makes work feel complicated for you right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Well, I mean, it’s just... there are times when I feel like I’m being watched or judged, especially after that whole thing with HR. It’s hard to shake off that feeling, you know? Like, I’m always worried that if I need to take time off for my health, people will think less of me. So, I just try to keep my head down and not draw attention. It just feels safer that way.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like the experience with HR has really impacted how you feel at work, creating a sense of being scrutinized and judged. That must be really stressful, especially when you’re trying to manage your health. Keeping your head down to avoid attention seems like a way to cope with that pressure. How does that affect your day-to-day experience at work?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, it definitely makes things harder. I find myself second-guessing everything I do. Like, I’ll hesitate before speaking up in meetings or sharing ideas because I worry about how they’ll be received. It’s like I’m constantly on edge, trying to figure out if I’m saying the right thing or if people are judging me. It’s exhausting, honestly. I just want to do my job without feeling like I’m under a microscope all the time.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds really exhausting, feeling like you have to constantly monitor yourself and worry about how others perceive you. It must be draining to second-guess your contributions and feel that pressure in meetings. I can see how that would take away from your ability to focus on your work. What do you think might help you feel a little more at ease in those situations?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I don’t know... I guess it would help if I felt more secure in my position, like if I knew that my contributions were valued no matter what. But it’s hard to shake that feeling of being judged. Maybe if I could just... talk to someone about it, but then again, I worry about how that would come across. I don’t want to seem like I can’t handle things. It’s just... frustrating. I wish I could feel more confident, but it’s tough when you’re always looking over your shoulder.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It makes sense that feeling secure in your position would help alleviate some of that pressure. It sounds like you’re caught between wanting to express your feelings and concerns, but also fearing how that might be perceived. That frustration of wanting to feel confident while constantly looking over your shoulder must be really challenging. Have you had any experiences in the past where you felt more secure or confident in similar situations?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Um, not really. I mean, there have been times when I felt okay, but they were pretty rare. Most of the time, I just remember feeling like I had to keep everything to myself. I guess I’ve always been more of a “stay in the background” type. It’s just easier that way. I don’t want to rock the boat or make things worse for myself. So, I try to just get through the day without drawing attention. It’s not ideal, but it’s what I know.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like staying in the background has become a familiar way for you to navigate your work environment, even if it doesn’t feel ideal. I can see how that pattern might feel safer, but it also seems to limit your ability to express yourself and feel valued. It’s understandable to want to avoid rocking the boat, especially if you’ve felt that way for a long time. How do you feel about that approach of staying in the background? Does it bring you any comfort, or does it also come with its own frustrations?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I guess it’s a bit of both. On one hand, it feels safer, like I’m not putting myself out there to be judged. But on the other hand, it’s frustrating because I know I have ideas and things to contribute. I just... don’t feel like they matter enough to share. It’s like I’m stuck in this cycle where I want to be more involved, but I’m too worried about how it’ll be received. So, I end up feeling kind of invisible, which isn’t great either. It’s just... complicated.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That does sound complicated, feeling torn between the safety of staying under the radar and the desire to share your ideas and be more involved. It’s understandable to feel invisible when you’re holding back, especially when you know you have valuable contributions to make. It seems like there’s a conflict between wanting to be seen and the fear of judgment. How do you typically cope with those feelings of invisibility when they come up?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I try to keep busy with my tasks, you know? Just focus on getting things done and not think too much about how I’m feeling. Sometimes I’ll just zone out and do my work without really engaging with others. It’s easier that way. I guess I also try to remind myself that it’s okay to just get through the day without making waves. But deep down, I know it’s not really solving anything. It’s just... a way to cope, I suppose.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like keeping busy and zoning out helps you manage those feelings, even if it’s not a long-term solution. Focusing on tasks can provide a sense of control, but it also seems like it might leave you feeling disconnected from your colleagues and your own needs. It’s understandable to want to avoid making waves, but I can see how that might lead to a sense of frustration or emptiness over time. What do you think it would take for you to feel more comfortable engaging with others at work?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I don’t know... maybe if I felt like there was a safe space to share my thoughts without being judged? Like, if I knew that my ideas would be welcomed and appreciated, I might feel more comfortable speaking up. But it’s hard to imagine that happening when I’ve felt so judged in the past. I guess it would also help if I had some support from colleagues or a mentor who could encourage me. But I’m not sure how to find that or if it’s even possible. It just feels... overwhelming to think about.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It makes sense that having a safe space to share your thoughts and feeling supported by colleagues or a mentor would help you feel more comfortable engaging. It’s understandable to feel overwhelmed when thinking about how to create that environment, especially after experiencing judgment in the past. It sounds like you’re yearning for connection and validation, which is completely natural. What do you think might be a small step you could take toward finding that support or creating a more welcoming space for yourself?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I guess... maybe I could try to reach out to someone I trust a little more? Like, just to have a casual conversation and see how they feel about things at work. It’s a bit scary, though. I worry about how they might react or if they’ll think I’m overreacting. But I suppose if I could find someone who feels the same way, it might help. It’s just hard to take that first step, you know? I keep thinking about all the “what ifs.”

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Reaching out to someone you trust sounds like a thoughtful step, even if it feels scary. It’s natural to have those “what ifs” running through your mind, especially when you’re considering opening up about your feelings. It seems like finding common ground with someone could help you feel less alone in your experiences. What do you think might help you manage those fears or “what ifs” as you consider taking that first step?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think... maybe I could remind myself that it’s okay to feel nervous about it. Everyone has their own struggles, and maybe they’d appreciate someone opening up to them. I could also try to focus on the potential positive outcomes instead of just the negatives. Like, if it goes well, I might feel more connected and supported. I guess writing down my thoughts beforehand could help too, just to organize what I want to say. It’s just a matter of finding the courage to actually do it, I suppose.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Those sound like really constructive strategies! Acknowledging your nerves and reminding yourself that it’s okay to feel that way can help ease some of the pressure. Focusing on the potential positive outcomes and organizing your thoughts beforehand could also give you a sense of clarity and confidence. It’s understandable that finding the courage to take that step can be challenging. What do you think might help you build that courage as you prepare to reach out?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think maybe setting a small goal for myself could help. Like, just telling myself that I’ll reach out to one person this week, even if it’s just a casual chat. I could also remind myself that it’s a low-pressure situation; I don’t have to dive into everything all at once. Just starting with something simple might make it feel less daunting. And maybe I could talk to myself a bit, like, encouraging myself to take that step. It’s just... I have to keep reminding myself that it’s worth a shot.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Setting a small, manageable goal sounds like a great approach! It can help make the process feel less overwhelming and more achievable. Focusing on a low-pressure situation and taking it one step at a time can really ease the burden. Encouraging yourself along the way is also a powerful tool. It seems like you’re starting to find a way to navigate this in a way that feels more comfortable for you. How do you feel about this plan as you think about moving forward?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel... a little more hopeful, I guess? It’s still a bit nerve-wracking, but having a plan makes it feel more doable. I like the idea of taking it one step at a time instead of trying to tackle everything at once. It’s nice to think that maybe I could find some support and not feel so alone in all of this. I just hope I can actually follow through and not let my fears get in the way. But I suppose that’s part of the process, right?

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s wonderful to hear that you’re feeling a bit more hopeful! Having a plan and breaking it down into smaller steps can really make a difference. It’s completely normal to feel nervous about following through, but recognizing that it’s part of the process is an important insight. Allowing yourself to feel those fears while still taking steps forward can be a powerful way to build resilience. What do you think you might do to keep that sense of hope and motivation as you move forward with your plan?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I could keep reminding myself of the reasons why I want to reach out in the first place. Like, wanting to feel more connected and supported at work. Maybe I could also write down some positive affirmations or reminders to look at when I’m feeling uncertain. And I guess talking about it here helps too, like having someone to share my thoughts with. I’ll try to hold onto that feeling of hope and not let the doubts take over. It’s just... a matter of staying focused on what I want to achieve, I suppose.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Those are excellent strategies! Keeping your reasons for reaching out at the forefront of your mind and using positive affirmations can really help reinforce your motivation. It’s great that you recognize the value of sharing your thoughts here as well. Staying focused on your goals and holding onto that sense of hope can make a significant difference as you navigate this process. As you prepare to take these steps, how do you feel about checking in with yourself along the way to see how you’re doing?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think that could be really helpful. Checking in with myself might help me stay grounded and aware of how I’m feeling as I go through this. I could set aside a little time each week to reflect on how things are going, what’s working, and what’s still challenging. It might also help me adjust my approach if I need to. I guess it’s just about being kind to myself and recognizing that it’s okay to have ups and downs along the way. That feels like a good way to keep moving forward.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a thoughtful plan! Setting aside time to reflect on your experiences can help you stay connected to your feelings and progress. Being kind to yourself and acknowledging that there will be ups and downs is an important part of the journey. It seems like you’re developing a strong foundation for moving forward with this. As you think about your next steps, is there anything else you’d like to explore or discuss today?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Um, I think I’ve covered a lot of what’s been on my mind. It feels good to have a plan and some strategies to work with. I guess I just hope I can keep this momentum going and not get too caught up in my fears again. But I appreciate having this space to talk about it. I’m not sure if there’s anything else right now, but I’ll definitely keep these thoughts in mind as I move forward. Thank you for listening.
